narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the 4th decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—below the typical student's 6th or 5th decile position—along with your LSAT score of 32, which is lower than the average of about 37. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 stands out as stronger than the typical 3.21, and your upper-middle family income bracket is a solid foundation compared to most applicants. You're also planning full-time studies, which is the norm, and as a white female applicant, your profile aligns closely with the majority in our dataset.

The model's decision was driven primarily by a few key factors from your background, ranked by their influence. Your third-year school decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting a bar failure risk. This was closely followed by your LSAT score, which also nudged the prediction in that direction, and then your first-year decile, which played a similar role. Your family income level came next in influence, pushing toward higher risk, while your undergraduate GPA had a moderate effect in the same way, despite being above average for this student. Your racial background contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, your full-time status had minimal impact, and notably, your gender as female was the one factor that slightly favored a passing prediction.

In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your lower deciles and LSAT score stand out as bigger drags than for most, while your GPA gives you an edge that others lack. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales to that 41% failure probability, even though no single issue was overwhelming.

Looking ahead, you can realistically boost your future bar exam odds by focusing on what you can control: retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average score, as it's a strong predictor here, and building stronger study habits early in law school to climb those decile rankings in future years. Your solid GPA shows you have the academic capability—channel that into targeted LSAT prep or bar-focused practice now. With these steps, a reapplication could look much stronger next time. Keep pushing; many students turn predictions like this around with focused effort.
